description: Go baseline invariants: no TLS verification bypass, no fmt.Sprintf into database Query/Exec/Prepare, no `sh -c` shell hand-off via os/exec, weak-hash imports flagged. Deliberately conservative — only patterns that are nearly always violations. The fmt.Sprintf-SQL rule overlaps the engine's built-in sql-interpolation (the floor) but is kept here so it fires even when this pack is vendored alone. Extended for SECENG-736 with: go-math-rand-for-tokens, go-context-todo, gosec-g104-globally-excluded, go-tls-insecure-skip-verify-test, go-strings-tolower-secret, go-pkg-errors-deprecated, go-panic-in-production, go-errors-new-with-sprintf, go-ioutil-deprecated, go-des-rc4-crypto.

rules.message: InsecureSkipVerify: true disables TLS certificate validation — issue a properly-signed cert or pin a CA, never bypass verification

rules.//: Catches fmt.Sprintf used directly as the SQL argument in a Query/Exec/Prepare call on the same line. Note: the line-by-line linter cannot detect multi-line calls where the method opener is on an unmodified line and only the fmt.Sprintf argument line is new in the diff — treat as a known scope limitation and rely on code review for that pattern.
rules.message: fmt.Sprintf passed to a database Query/Exec/Prepare call — use bound parameters ($1/?), never string-built SQL
exec.Command("sh", "-c"|"-lc"|"-ec", ...) hands the command line to a shell — call the binary directly with an argv slice (no shell, no interpolation)
import of crypto/md5 or crypto/sha1 — both are broken for security use; ok for non-security checksums (etags, content addressing) but confirm the use case

rules.//: math/rand is a deterministic PRNG seeded from a small state — never for tokens, ids, key material. crypto/rand is the only correct source for security-sensitive randomness. Test files (path contains _test.go) are matched too — add `//nolint:gosec` with a one-line justification at the use site if the test genuinely needs deterministic randomness.
rules.message: math/rand is deterministic and predictable — for tokens, UUIDs, session ids, or any security-sensitive randomness use crypto/rand. If this is a non-security use (load shedding, jitter, fuzz), add `//nolint:gosec` with a one-line justification at the import site.

rules.//: context.TODO() is a stub for `we haven't plumbed the context yet`. Shipping it to production means the operation can't be canceled and will leak goroutines on shutdown. Warn-level so a deliberate use can be justified with //nolint.
rules.message: context.TODO() committed — replace with the real upstream context or context.Background() at a clear root.

rules.//: Global gosec G104 suppression silences all unchecked-error findings, including security-sensitive ones (e.g. ignored rand.Read, ignored crypto/hmac.Write). The pattern matches a YAML list-item that IS exactly G104 (`- G104`). WARN-level (not HARD) because `- G104` can also appear under an `includes:` key to ENABLE the check; a single-line regex cannot reliably distinguish exclusion context from inclusion context. Review flagged lines manually — if the `- G104` is under an `issues: exclude-rules:` or a global `suppressions:` block, move it to a path-scoped `exclusions.rules` filter instead. If it is under `includes:` to enable the check, the warning is a false positive and can be ignored.
rules.message: G104 appears as a bare YAML list item in golangci config — verify whether this is suppressing G104 (bad: silences unchecked-error security findings) or enabling it (fine). If suppressing, move to `exclusions.rules` with a `path: _test\\.go$` filter; add per-site `//nolint:gosec // G104: <reason>` where deliberate. If enabling, this warning is a false positive.

rules.//: Warn-level companion to go-tls-insecure-skip-verify: test files that set InsecureSkipVerify=true should document why (local self-signed cert) so reviewers can confirm the scope is intentional.
rules.message: tls.Config.InsecureSkipVerify = true in a test file — acceptable for local self-signed servers, but add a comment explaining the cert setup so reviewers can confirm scope.

rules.//: Case-folding secret material before comparison weakens it: strings.ToLower(password) collapses 26 distinct uppercase characters into their lowercase equivalents, halving the effective alphabet for that position. Worse, it often signals a buggy case-insensitive comparison of credentials (which should be byte-exact, constant-time). Pattern catches `strings.ToLower(...password...)`, `...secret...`, `...token...`, `...api_key/apikey/api-key...` on a single line. Case-insensitive (`i` flag) so PASSWORD/Password/password all match. Fix: compare with subtle.ConstantTimeCompare on the raw bytes; do NOT case-fold credentials.
rules.message: strings.ToLower applied to secret-like material (password/secret/token/api_key) — case-folding weakens the comparison and is almost always a bug. Compare credentials byte-exact with subtle.ConstantTimeCompare; case-fold only non-secret identifiers.

rules.//: github.com/pkg/errors is deprecated since Go 1.13 — the standard library's fmt.Errorf with the %w verb (and errors.Is/errors.As/errors.Unwrap) is the modern idiom. Mixing both leads to confusing wrap chains and surprising behavior under errors.Is. Fix: replace `errors.Wrap(err, "ctx")` with `fmt.Errorf("ctx: %w", err)`, `errors.WithStack` with bare returns or fmt.Errorf, and update imports.
rules.message: github.com/pkg/errors is deprecated since Go 1.13 — use stdlib fmt.Errorf with %w and errors.Is/As/Unwrap. Replace `errors.Wrap(err, msg)` with `fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", msg, err)`.

rules.//: panic() in production code crashes the whole process — fine for impossible-state assertions, wrong for normal errors that should propagate as `error` values. The pattern matches lines that START with whitespace then `panic(` — i.e. a panic inside a function body, not a top-level `var _ = panic(...)`. WARN-level because test files and legitimate `panic` (init-time invariant checks, recovered worker boundaries) also match — opt out per-site with `//nolint:govet // SAFE: <reason>` explaining why a panic is correct here.
rules.message: panic() inside a function body — for normal error paths return an `error` instead; panic should be reserved for impossible-state assertions and recovered worker boundaries. If this panic is intentional (init-time check, assertion), add `//nolint:govet // SAFE: <reason>` on the same line. Test files match too — same opt-out.

rules.//: errors.New(fmt.Sprintf(...)) is a tell that someone wanted fmt.Errorf and reached for the wrong constructor. fmt.Errorf is the idiomatic form and, with %w, supports error wrapping that errors.New does not. Mechanical fix: replace `errors.New(fmt.Sprintf(format, args...))` with `fmt.Errorf(format, args...)`.
rules.message: errors.New(fmt.Sprintf(...)) — collapse to fmt.Errorf(...) which is idiomatic and supports %w error wrapping.

rules.//: io/ioutil was deprecated in Go 1.16; every symbol has a stdlib replacement: ioutil.ReadAll → io.ReadAll, ioutil.ReadFile/WriteFile → os.ReadFile/WriteFile, ioutil.ReadDir → os.ReadDir, ioutil.TempDir/TempFile → os.MkdirTemp/CreateTemp, ioutil.NopCloser → io.NopCloser, ioutil.Discard → io.Discard. New code should not import it.
rules.message: io/ioutil is deprecated since Go 1.16 — use io/os equivalents (io.ReadAll, os.ReadFile/WriteFile, os.ReadDir, os.MkdirTemp/CreateTemp, io.NopCloser, io.Discard).

rules.//: DES (56-bit key) and RC4 (broken stream cipher) are cryptographically dead — RC4 is forbidden by RFC 7465 in TLS, DES has been brute-forceable since the 1990s. No legitimate new use exists. Fix: AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 for symmetric encryption.
rules.message: crypto/des and crypto/rc4 are broken ciphers — DES has a 56-bit key (brute-forceable), RC4 is forbidden by RFC 7465. Use AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 (crypto/aes + crypto/cipher, or golang.org/x/crypto/chacha20poly1305).
